tier_2_handbook.definition: Cutaneous lymphomas are a heterogeneous group of extranodal B-cell and T-cell non-Hodgkin lymphomas (NHL) that originate in and are usually confined to the skin. They represent a broad spectrum of disorders with distinct clinical, pathological, and molecular features. The embryological origin of the skin-associated lymphoid tissue (SALT) includes lymphocytes that home to the skin. Anatomically, the skin is divided into body regions as defined by the ISCL/EORTC for staging purposes (e.g., head/neck, upper/lower arms, chest, abdomen/genital, upper/lower back/buttocks, and upper/lower legs). The disease entities covered in these guidelines are: Primary Cutaneous B-Cell Lymphomas (PCMZL, PCFCL, PCDLBCL leg type); Mycosis Fungoides (MF) and Sézary Syndrome (SS); Primary Cutaneous CD30+ T-Cell Lymphoproliferative Disorders (PC-ALCL, Lymphomatoid Papulosis); Subcutaneous Panniculitis-Like T-Cell Lymphoma (SPTCL); and Primary Cutaneous Indolent T-Cell Lymphoproliferative Disorders (PCSM-TLPD, PCA-TLPD). A multidisciplinary team approach is essential for diagnosis and care.

subtypes.name: Primary Cutaneous Marginal Zone Lymphoma (PCMZL)
subtypes.description: The second most common subtype of primary cutaneous B-cell lymphoma (PC-BCL), constituting 24%-31% of cases. It is classified as a lymphoproliferative disorder in the International Consensus Classification (ICC) due to its indolent course. Clinically, it presents as solitary or multiple erythematous to violaceous papules, small nodules, plaques, or tumors, most commonly on the trunk, upper extremities, and head. Histologically, it shows a nodular or diffuse infiltrate of small B-cells. Immunophenotypically, the cells are negative for CD10 and BCL6 but are often positive for CD20, CD79a, and BCL2. About one-third of cases may express IgG4. It can be subdivided into two prognostic groups based on Ig heavy chain usage: a common Ig class-switched subtype (IgG, IgA, IgE) and a less common IgM-positive subtype.
key_associations: Excellent prognosis (5-year overall survival rate of 99%)
Relapses in the skin occur in 50% of patients
May be associated with chronic antigen stimulation

subtypes.name: Primary Cutaneous Follicle Center Lymphoma (PCFCL)
subtypes.description: The most common subtype of PC-BCL, accounting for 57% of cases. It typically presents as solitary, firm, pink to violaceous papules, nodules, plaques, or tumors, primarily on the scalp, face, forehead, and trunk. Multifocal skin lesions are seen in 15% of cases, and ulceration is rare. Dissemination to extracutaneous sites is extremely uncommon. Histologically, it shows a follicular or diffuse growth pattern. Immunophenotypically, the cells express CD20, CD79a, and BCL6, with surface Ig light chain positivity but negative cytoplasmic Ig light chain. BCL2 is usually negative or minimally expressed, and CD10 can be negative in diffuse growth patterns. A germinal center B-cell (GCB) phenotype is most frequent. Cutaneous recurrences occur near the initial site in approximately 30% of cases.
key_associations: t(14;18) translocation is rare and suggests systemic disease

subtypes.name: Primary Cutaneous Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma, Leg Type (PC-DLBCL, LT)
subtypes.description: The rarest subtype of PC-BCL, comprising 11%-19% of cases and 4% of all primary cutaneous lymphomas. It typically presents as red to bluish plaques or tumors on one or both legs, but can occur at other sites (10%-15% of cases). Lesions can ulcerate. Histologically, it consists of diffuse sheets of large B-cells. Immunophenotypically, the cells express CD20, CD79a, strong BCL2, IRF4/MUM1, FOXP1, IgM, and occasionally MYC. CD10 is usually negative. It most commonly has an activated B-cell (ABC) phenotype and belongs to the MCD subtype, with co-occurring MYD88 and CD79B mutations. It is usually aggressive with a high frequency of extracutaneous relapses and a poor prognosis.
subtypes.frequency: 11%-19% of primary cutaneous B-cell lymphomas; 4% of all primary cutaneous lymphomas
key_associations: Poor prognosis (5-year overall survival rate is 50%)
Inactivation of CDKN2A associated with inferior prognosis

subtypes.name: Mycosis Fungoides (MF)
subtypes.description: The most common cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL). Most patients exhibit an indolent clinical course with intermittent, stable, or slow progression of skin lesions, which are patch, plaque, or tumor stage. Extracutaneous involvement (lymph nodes, blood, or less commonly other organs) may be seen in advanced stages. It is characterized by a neoplastic proliferation of skin-resident effector memory T cells with a typical CD2+, CD3+, CD4+, CD8- immunophenotype. Large-cell transformation (LCT) is defined histologically as >25% large tumor cells and is associated with a more aggressive course. Several clinicopathologic variants exist, including folliculotropic MF, hypopigmented MF, pagetoid reticulosis, and granulomatous slack skin.
subtypes.frequency: Most common subtype of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma

subtypes.name: Sézary Syndrome (SS)
subtypes.description: The leukemic variant of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, characterized by the triad of erythroderma (≥80% BSA), generalized lymphadenopathy, and the presence of atypical T cells (Sézary cells) in the peripheral blood. It is rare, accounting for <5% of cutaneous lymphomas, and predominantly affects older individuals. The blood involvement is defined as B2 level: ≥1000/µL of CD4+/CD26- or CD4+/CD7- cells or another aberrant population identified by flow cytometry. It is thought to arise from thymic memory T cells, distinct from the skin-resident cells of origin of MF. SS carries a poor prognosis.

subtypes.name: Primary Cutaneous Anaplastic Large Cell Lymphoma (PC-ALCL)
subtypes.description: Represents about 8% of cutaneous lymphoma cases. It typically follows an indolent course with cutaneous relapses common but excellent prognosis maintained. Histologically, it is characterized by diffuse, cohesive sheets of large CD30-positive (>75% of cells) cells with anaplastic, pleomorphic, or immunoblastic appearance. Clinically, it usually presents as solitary or localized nodules or tumors, which are often ulcerated; multifocal lesions occur in about 20% of cases. Extracutaneous disease occurs in about 10% of cases, usually involving regional lymph nodes. ALK is typically negative and the t(2;5) translocation absent.

subtypes.name: Lymphomatoid Papulosis (LyP)
subtypes.description: A chronic, recurrent, spontaneously regressing papulonodular skin disorder that is part of the spectrum of CD30+ T-cell lymphoproliferative disorders. It is classified as an LPD due to its benign clinical behavior despite a malignant histologic appearance. It is characterized by histologically heterogeneous lesions with large atypical anaplastic, immunoblastic, or Hodgkin-like cells in a marked inflammatory background. Multiple histologic subtypes (A-F) have been defined. While it has an excellent prognosis (5- and 10-year overall survival rates of 92%), it is associated with an increased risk of developing other lymphomas, such as MF, PC-ALCL, systemic ALCL, or Hodgkin lymphoma.
subtypes.frequency: Common; part of the CD30+ LPD spectrum

subtypes.name: Subcutaneous Panniculitis-Like T-Cell Lymphoma (SPTCL)
subtypes.description: A rare, indolent subtype of CTCL characterized by mature, medium-sized, CD8+ activated T cells with tropism for adipocytes in the subcutaneous tissue. Patients tend to be young, often with a personal or family history of autoimmune disorders like SLE. The presentation includes non-ulcerated deep nodules, typically on the legs, which may resolve with lipodystrophy and hyperpigmentation. Fever and malaise are common. A major complication is hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH), which is associated with severe morbidity or mortality. It is defined as an alpha-beta T-cell lymphoma; the gamma-delta variant is a separate entity (primary cutaneous gamma-delta T-cell lymphoma).

subtypes.name: Primary Cutaneous CD4+ Small/Medium T-Cell Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PCSM-TLPD)
subtypes.description: A newly defined indolent LPD, reclassified from primary cutaneous lymphomas due to its excellent prognosis. It typically presents as a solitary, asymptomatic nodule or plaque on the head/neck, trunk, or upper extremities. Histologically, it shows a superficial band-like or nodular/diffuse infiltrate of predominantly small/medium-sized CD4+ T cells, often with a reactive infiltrate. Immunophenotypically, it is CD3+, CD4+, CD8-, PD-1+, ICOS+, and often BCL6+/-. It has a T-follicular helper (TFH) phenotype. It demonstrates frequent spontaneous remission and carries an excellent prognosis with a 5-year overall survival rate of 100%.

subtypes.name: Primary Cutaneous CD8+ Acral T-Cell Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PCA-TLPD)
subtypes.description: Another indolent LPD reclassified from primary cutaneous lymphomas. It typically presents as a solitary, asymptomatic papule, nodule, or plaque, most commonly on acral sites like the ear or nose. Histologically, it shows a dermal, non-epidermotropic, diffuse or nodular infiltrate of atypical medium-sized CD8+ T cells. Immunophenotypically, it is CD3+, CD4-, CD8+, TIA1+, and granzyme B+/-. Spontaneous remission is less common than in PCSM-TLPD but can occur. It carries an excellent prognosis with a 5-year overall survival rate of 100%.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Molecular pathogenesis varies significantly by subtype. In primary cutaneous B-cell lymphomas: PCFCL is characterized by a germinal center B-cell (GCB) phenotype and typically lacks MYD88 and CD79B mutations. PCMZL can be divided by Ig heavy chain usage (Ig class-switched vs. IgM+) with different prognoses. PCDLBCL, leg type is characterized by an activated B-cell (ABC) phenotype and the MCD subtype, with high prevalence of gain-of-function mutations in MYD88 L265P and CD79B. Inactivation of CDKN2A and MYD88 L265P mutations are associated with inferior prognosis in this subtype. In cutaneous T-cell lymphomas: MF and SS are driven by the clonal proliferation of skin-resident (MF) or thymic memory (SS) T cells. Large-cell transformation (LCT) is defined by >25% large cells. In primary cutaneous CD30+ T-cell LPDs, DUSP22-IRF4 gene rearrangement has been described in PC-ALCL and LyP but is not prognostic. A subset of CTCL may carry activating JAK2 rearrangements. In SPTCL, germline mutations in HAVCR2 (encoding TIM-3) are frequent (25%-85%) and are associated with HLH and may be more prevalent in patients of Asian ancestry. Recurrent mutations in epigenetic modifiers (KMT2C, KMT2D) and the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway (PLCG1, ARID1B) have also been identified. The absence of common lymphoma driver mutations in SPTCL suggests an immune dysregulation process.

epidemiology.incidence: The incidence data from a SEER database population-based analysis (2001-2005) of 3884 cases showed cutaneous B-cell lymphomas (CBCL) accounted for 29% and cutaneous T-cell lymphomas (CTCL) for 71% of primary cutaneous lymphomas. An updated SEER analysis (2000-2018) reported 14,942 CTCL diagnoses in the US, with MF being the most common, followed by PC-ALCL. An overall increase in CTCL incidence was reported over this period. Specific incidence rates by subtype or demographics are not provided in the source text beyond these proportions.
epidemiology.mortality: Mortality varies greatly by subtype and stage. For example, PCFCL and PCMZL have 5-year overall survival (OS) rates >95% and ~99%, respectively, while PCDLBCL, leg type has a 5-year OS rate of ~50%. For MF, prognosis is highly stage-dependent, with early skin-limited disease having an excellent prognosis and advanced-stage disease having a poorer outcome. Sézary syndrome carries a poor prognosis.
epidemiology.trends: A SEER database analysis showed an overall increase in the incidence of primary cutaneous T-cell lymphoma in the United States from 2000 to 2018. An improvement in survival for patients with PCDLBCL, leg type in France has been reported.
epidemiology.demographics: Sézary syndrome predominantly affects older individuals. SPTCL tends to affect young patients, with a slight bias for females, and may have a personal or family history of autoimmune disorders like SLE. HAVCR2 mutations in SPTCL appear more prevalent in patients of Asian ancestry. Specific racial/ethnic incidence disparities beyond this are not detailed in the source.

risk_factors.detail: Patients with SPTCL often have a personal or family history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) or other autoimmune disorders.
risk_factors.strength: Strong association mentioned in context of SPTCL

risk_factors.detail: Germline mutations in the HAVCR2 gene (encoding TIM-3) are found in 25%-85% of SPTCL cases and are associated with hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) and a more severe disease course. They are more prevalent in patients of Asian ancestry.
risk_factors.strength: Strong predisposing factor for SPTCL with HLH

risk_factors.detail: HTLV-1 serology is recommended to exclude adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL), which can mimic cutaneous lymphomas. ATLL is associated with HTLV-1 infection.
risk_factors.strength: Causal for ATLL; relevant differential for cutaneous lymphomas

risk_factors.detail: Patients on immunosuppressive therapy are at increased risk for UV radiation-associated skin malignancies, which is a consideration when using phototherapy for cutaneous lymphomas.
risk_factors.strength: Modifying factor for secondary malignancy risk during treatment

risk_factors.detail: MF and SS can occur at any age but are more common in older adults. Sézary syndrome predominantly affects older individuals. For PCDLBCL, leg type, age is a factor in treatment selection.
Lymphomatoid drug reactions, which can mimic CD30+ T-cell LPDs, have been linked to drugs such as amlodipine, carbamazepine, cefuroxime, and valsartan.
risk_factors.strength: Known association for a differential diagnosis

protective_factors: Not explicitly mentioned in the source text for cutaneous lymphomas.

PCFCL_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Primary cutaneous follicle center lymphoma (PCFCL) typically presents as solitary, firm, pink to violaceous papules, nodules, plaques, or tumors, predominantly located on the scalp, face, forehead, or trunk. Lesions are usually asymptomatic, though pruritus may occur. Multifocal skin lesions are seen in approximately 15% of cases. Ulceration is rare. PCFCL follows an indolent clinical course with an excellent prognosis, with a 5-year overall survival rate exceeding 95%. Cutaneous recurrences occur near the initial site in about 30% of cases, but extracutaneous dissemination is extremely uncommon [1,2].

symptoms.name: Asymptomatic skin lesions
symptoms.detail: Most patients present with asymptomatic solitary or multiple skin-colored to violaceous papules, nodules, or plaques.

symptoms.name: Pruritus
symptoms.detail: Mild itching may be present but is not a prominent feature.

signs.name: Solitary or grouped skin lesions
signs.detail: Firm, non-tender, pink to violaceous papules, nodules, plaques, or tumors, often on sun-exposed areas.

signs.name: Multifocal lesions
signs.detail: Involvement of multiple skin sites, seen in 15% of cases.

atypical_or_missed: Presentation with diffuse or ulcerated lesions may lead to misdiagnosis as other cutaneous lymphomas or inflammatory disorders.
Overlap with other indolent B-cell lymphoproliferative disorders can delay definitive diagnosis.

PCFCL_red_flags: Systemic symptoms (e.g., fever, night sweats, weight loss)
Abnormal laboratory studies (e.g., elevated LDH, cytopenias)

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for histopathologic and immunophenotypic diagnosis; shave biopsy is inadequate due to dermal infiltrate.
diagnostic.key_findings: Diffuse or nodular infiltrate of B cells with germinal center phenotype; immunophenotype: CD20+, CD79a+, BCL6+, CD10−/+, BCL2−/minimally expressed; GCB subtype [1,3].

diagnostic.rationale: Establishes lymphoma subtype and rules out differential diagnoses.
diagnostic.key_findings: Panel may include CD20, CD3, CD10, BCL2, BCL6, IRF4/MUM1, Ki-67, CD5, CD43, CD21, CD23, cyclin D1, kappa/lambda, MYC (IHC or ISH) [4].

diagnostic.rationale: To detect genetic abnormalities and rule out systemic follicular lymphoma.
diagnostic.key_findings: t(14;18) if systemic FL suspected; BCL2 and BCL6 rearrangements if MYC positive [4].

diagnostic.rationale: For MYD88 and CD79B mutations to distinguish from PC-DLBCL, leg type.
diagnostic.key_findings: MYD88 L265P mutation is absent in PCFCL [4].

staging.test: Integrated whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or C/A/P CT with contrast
staging.rationale: Assesses for extracutaneous involvement; may be omitted if clinically indicated in indolent disease.
staging.indication: All patients at diagnosis to rule out systemic disease [4].

staging.rationale: Assesses organ function and detects metabolic abnormalities.

biomarkers.rationale: Differentiates PCFCL from systemic FL and other B-cell lymphomas.
Absence supports primary cutaneous origin; presence suggests systemic FL.

do_not_miss: Histopathologic review by pathologist with expertise in cutaneous lymphoma
avoid: Routine bone marrow biopsy (limited value in indolent PCFCL without cytopenias)

PCFCL_staging.system: TNM classification of cutaneous lymphoma other than MF/SS (CUTB-A) [5]

t_stages.description: Solitary lesion: T1A (<5 cm diameter), T1B (≥5 cm diameter)
Multiple lesions limited to 1 body region or 2 contiguous body regions: T2A (all disease <15 cm diameter), T2B (all disease 15 to <30 cm diameter), T2C (all disease ≥30 cm diameter)
Generalized skin involvement: T3A (multiple lesions involving 2 noncontiguous body regions), T3B (multiple lesions involving ≥3 body regions)

n_stages.description: No clinical or pathologic lymph node involvement
Involvement of 1 peripheral lymph node region that drains an area of current or prior skin involvement: biopsy positive for lymphoma
Involvement of >2 peripheral lymph node regions or involvement of any lymph node region that does not drain an area of current or prior skin involvement: biopsy positive for lymphoma
Involvement of central lymph nodes: biopsy positive for lymphoma
Clinically abnormal peripheral or central lymph node but no pathologic determination

m_stages.description: Visceral involvement neither confirmed nor refuted

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Excellent prognosis with 5-year OS >95%; treated with local therapy.

stage_groups.criteria: Multiple lesions in 1 or 2 contiguous regions
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Excellent prognosis; may recur but confined to skin.
Excellent prognosis; management with skin-directed or systemic therapy.
Rare; manage according to systemic follicular lymphoma guidelines.

staging_pearls: Body regions are defined based on regional lymph node drainage patterns (see CUTB-A 2 of 2).
Bone marrow biopsy is not routinely required but may be considered for unexplained cytopenias or suspicion of aggressive subtypes.

PCMZL_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Primary cutaneous marginal zone lymphoma (PCMZL) typically presents as solitary or multiple erythematous to violaceous papules, small nodules, plaques, or tumors, primarily on the trunk, upper extremities, and head. Lesions are often asymptomatic and follow an indolent course with excellent prognosis (5-year survival rate 99%). Relapses in the skin occur in 50% of patients, but extracutaneous dissemination is uncommon [1,2].

symptoms.name: Asymptomatic skin lesions
symptoms.detail: Papules, nodules, or plaques that are usually non-tender.

signs.name: Solitary or multifocal skin lesions
signs.detail: Erythematous to violaceous papules or nodules, often on sun-exposed skin.

atypical_or_missed: Overlap with other cutaneous B-cell lymphomas or inflammatory conditions may delay diagnosis.
Plaque or tumor presentation may be mistaken for more aggressive lymphomas.

PCMZL_red_flags: Rapid growth or ulceration of lesions

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for histopathologic and immunophenotypic diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: Nodular infiltrate of small B cells with marginal zone differentiation; immunophenotype: CD20+, CD79a+, BCL2+, CD10−, BCL6−; may express IgG4 [1,6].

diagnostic.rationale: Establishes subtype and rules out other lymphomas.
diagnostic.key_findings: CD20, CD3, CD5, CD10, BCL2, BCL6, IRF4/MUM1, kappa/lambda [4].

diagnostic.test: Molecular studies for IgH gene rearrangement
diagnostic.rationale: To assess clonality and subclassify based on Ig heavy chain usage.
diagnostic.key_findings: Clonal IgH rearrangement; CXCR3-negative, Ig class-switched subtype (IgG/IgA/IgE) with better prognosis [7,8].

staging.test: C/A/P CT with contrast and/or FDG-PET/CT
staging.rationale: May be omitted if clinically indicated in indolent disease.
staging.indication: All patients to rule out extracutaneous involvement [4].

biomarkers.rationale: Prognostic stratification based on Ig subclass.

do_not_miss: Expert pathologic review for accurate subtype classification
avoid: Routine bone marrow biopsy without indication

PCMZL_staging.system: TNM classification of cutaneous lymphoma other than MF/SS (CUTB-A) [5]
PCMZL_staging.m_stages: As per PCFCL staging (M0, M1, MX)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Excellent prognosis; treat with local therapy.
Excellent prognosis; may require systemic therapy.
Rare; manage as nodal MZL.

staging_pearls: PCMZL is now classified as a clonal chronic lymphoproliferative disorder in some classifications.
Ig class-switched subtype has better prognosis.

PCDLBCL_leg_type_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Primary cutaneous diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, leg type (PC-DLBCL, leg type) typically presents as red to bluish plaques or tumors on one or both legs, which can ulcerate. Lesions are often painful. It is the rarest subtype of PC-BCL (11%–19%) and is usually aggressive with a poor prognosis (5-year OS rate 50%). Multiple skin lesions, inactivation of CDKN2A, and MYD88 L265P mutation are associated with inferior prognosis [1,2].

symptoms.name: Pain or tenderness of skin lesions

signs.name: Red to bluish plaques or tumors
signs.detail: Often on legs, but can occur elsewhere (10%–15%).

atypical_or_missed: Presentation on non-leg sites may lead to misdiagnosis.
Overlap with other large B-cell lymphomas or inflammatory conditions.

PCDLBCL_leg_type_red_flags: Systemic symptoms (e.g., fever, night sweats, weight loss)

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: Diffuse infiltrate of large B cells with ABC phenotype; immunophenotype: CD20+, CD79a+, BCL2 strong+, IRF4/MUM1+, FOXP1+, IgM+, CD10− [1,4].

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm subtype and distinguish from PCFCL.
diagnostic.key_findings: CD20, CD3, CD5, CD10, BCL2, BCL6, IRF4/MUM1, FOXP1, MYC, Ki-67 [4].

diagnostic.rationale: MYD88 L265P mutation is specific and associated with worse prognosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: MYD88 L265P mutation present in ~60% of cases [4].

staging.test: Integrated whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or C/A/P CT with contrast
staging.rationale: Essential due to aggressive nature and risk of extracutaneous spread.
To assess for bone marrow involvement.

PCDLBCL_leg_type_staging.system: TNM classification of cutaneous lymphoma other than MF/SS (CUTB-A) [5]
PCDLBCL_leg_type_staging.m_stages: As per PCFCL staging (M0, M1, MX)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Aggressive; treat with chemoimmunotherapy ± RT.
Poor prognosis; systemic chemoimmunotherapy required.

staging_pearls: PC-DLBCL, leg type is considered a systemic lymphoma requiring aggressive therapy.
MYD88 mutation status may guide therapy.

MF_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Mycosis fungoides (MF) is the most common cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, typically presenting with indolent, chronic skin lesions. The classic presentation includes patches, plaques, or tumors on sun-protected areas (e.g., buttocks, trunk). Lesions may be hypopigmented or hyperpigmented. Early-stage disease often has an excellent prognosis, while advanced stages can involve lymph nodes, blood, or viscera. Folliculotropic MF (FMF) presents with folliculocentric papules or nodules, often on the head and neck, and may be associated with alopecia [1,9].

symptoms.name: Skin lesions
symptoms.alarm: Yes if rapid growth or ulceration

atypical_or_missed: Hypopigmented MF in children or dark-skinned individuals may be mistaken for vitiligo or pityriasis rosea.
FMF may present with alopecia and be misdiagnosed as alopecia areata.
Pagetoid reticulosis presents as a solitary plaque and may be confused with psoriasis.

MF_red_flags: Systemic symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss)

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosis; multiple biopsies may be needed.
diagnostic.key_findings: Epidermotropic atypical lymphocytes with cerebriform nuclei; IHC: CD2+, CD3+, CD5+, CD7−/+, CD4+, CD8− (rarely CD8+), CD30−/+ [1,9].

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm diagnosis and evaluate for LCT or variant.
diagnostic.key_findings: CD2, CD3, CD4, CD5, CD7, CD8, CD20, CD30 [1].

diagnostic.rationale: Supports diagnosis but not solely diagnostic.
diagnostic.key_findings: Clonal TRB and/or TRG rearrangements [1].

diagnostic.rationale: To assess blood involvement and quantify Sézary cells.
diagnostic.key_findings: Expanded CD4+ cells with aberrant phenotype (CD4/CD7− or CD4/CD26−); TRBC1 may help [1].

staging.test: Integrated whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or C/A/P CT with contrast
staging.rationale: For staging in ≥T2b or LCT or FMF, or with adenopathy.
staging.indication: All patients with ≥T2 disease or suspicious features [1].

staging.test: CBC with differential and flow cytometry

biomarkers.rationale: May guide therapy with brentuximab vedotin.
Loss may indicate resistance to mogamulizumab.
To rule out ATLL in endemic areas.

avoid: Over-reliance on TCR rearrangement alone for diagnosis

MF_staging.system: TNMB classification and clinical staging for MF and SS (ISCL/EORTC) [10]

t_stages.description: Patches, papules, or plaques covering <10% BSA: T1a (patch only), T1b (plaque/papule ± patch)
Patches, papules, or plaques covering ≥10% BSA: T2a (patch only), T2b (plaque ± patch)
One or more tumors ≥1 cm in diameter

n_stages.description: Pathology Dutch grade 1 or NCI LN0-2: clone negative or equivocal (N1A), clone positive and identical to skin (N1B)
Dutch grade 2, NCI LN3: clone negative or equivocal (N2A), clone positive and identical to skin (N2B)
Dutch grade 3-4, NCI LN4: clone negative or equivocal (N3A), clone positive and identical to skin (N3B)
Clinically abnormal lymph nodes without pathologic determination

m_stages.description: Visceral involvement: M1a (BM only), M1b (non-BM visceral)
Visceral involvement neither confirmed nor refuted

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Limited skin involvement; excellent prognosis.
Skin-only disease with ≥10% BSA; good prognosis.
Limited skin with involved lymph nodes; prognosis depends on nodal status.
Tumor stage; more aggressive, requires systemic therapy.
Erythrodermic disease without blood involvement; guarded prognosis.
Erythrodermic with low blood burden; guarded prognosis.
Sézary syndrome; poor prognosis.
Sézary or non-Sézary with extensive nodal involvement; poor prognosis.
Visceral disease; very poor prognosis.

staging_pearls: BSA assessment: patient's palm with digits ≈1% BSA.
Folliculotropism or LCT can occur at any stage and may require more aggressive therapy.
Blood involvement: B2 defines Sézary syndrome.

SS_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Sézary syndrome (SS) is the leukemic variant of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, characterized by erythroderma (confluent erythema ≥80% BSA), generalized lymphadenopathy, and significant blood involvement with malignant T cells (Sézary cells). It predominantly affects older individuals and is associated with pruritus, skin pain, and systemic symptoms. SS arises from thymic memory T cells and is biologically distinct from MF. Prognosis is generally poor with 5-year survival rates <50% [1,9].

symptoms.name: Fatigue, weight loss, night sweats
symptoms.detail: Systemic symptoms due to cytokine storm.

atypical_or_missed: Early erythroderma may be mistaken for other dermatoses (e.g., psoriasis, drug reaction).
Hypopigmented variant in early stage.

diagnostic.rationale: May show similar findings to MF but often subtle.
diagnostic.key_findings: Epidermotropic atypical lymphocytes; may need correlation with blood [1].

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosis and staging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Expanded CD4+ cells with aberrant phenotype (CD4/CD7− or CD4/CD26−); Sézary cell count ≥1000/µL or CD4:CD8 ratio >10 [1].

diagnostic.test: Clonal TCR gene rearrangement in blood
diagnostic.rationale: Confirm clonality and identical clone to skin.
diagnostic.key_findings: Clonal TRB/TRG rearrangement identical to skin [1].

staging.test: Integrated whole-body FDG-PET/CT or C/A/P CT
staging.rationale: To assess nodal and visceral involvement.

staging.test: CBC with differential, LDH, metabolic panel

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Classic SS with leukemic involvement.
Extensive nodal disease with or without blood involvement.

staging_pearls: SS is defined by B2 blood involvement and erythroderma.
Prognosis is worse than MF with similar skin stage.

LCT_MF_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Large-cell transformation (LCT) in mycosis fungoides is defined histologically as >25% large cells in the tumor cell infiltrate. It often presents as rapidly growing tumors or ulcerated lesions, sometimes with loss of previous patch/plaque morphology. LCT is associated with more aggressive disease and poorer prognosis, especially when occurring in advanced stages. CD30 expression may be present in 30%–50% of cases [1,11].
atypical_or_missed: May be misdiagnosed as other aggressive lymphomas if not in context of MF.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential to confirm LCT.
diagnostic.key_findings: >25% large cells; may be CD30+ [1].

diagnostic.rationale: To evaluate for transformation and guide therapy.
diagnostic.key_findings: CD30 expression common [1].

staging.rationale: To assess extent of disease and extracutaneous involvement.
To evaluate for SS or leukemic phase.

biomarkers.rationale: May guide therapy with brentuximab vedotin.

LCT_MF_staging.system: Same as MF staging, but LCT may upstage disease.
LCT_MF_staging.t_stages: As per MF (T0-T4); LCT often associated with T3.
LCT_MF_staging.stage_groups: LCT does not change stage grouping but indicates more aggressive biology and need for systemic therapy.
staging_pearls: LCT is a histologic finding, not a stage, but influences treatment intensity.
CD30 status may guide targeted therapy.

PCALCL_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Primary cutaneous anaplastic large cell lymphoma (PC-ALCL) typically presents as solitary or localized, often ulcerated nodules or tumors, most commonly on the head, neck, or trunk. Multifocal lesions occur in about 20% of cases. Spontaneous regression may occur. It follows an indolent course with excellent prognosis, although cutaneous relapses are common. Extracutaneous disease occurs in about 10% of cases, usually involving regional lymph nodes [1,12].
atypical_or_missed: Overlap with lymphomatoid papulosis or CD30+ MF transformation.
May be misdiagnosed as Hodgkin lymphoma if in lymph node.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: Diffuse sheets of large anaplastic cells with CD30 expression (>75%); immunophenotype: CD3+, CD4+, CD8−, CD56−, ALK− [1,12].

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm diagnosis and rule out other CD30+ disorders.
diagnostic.key_findings: CD3, CD4, CD8, CD20, CD30, CD56, ALK [1].

diagnostic.test: FISH for ALK and DUSP22 rearrangements
diagnostic.rationale: ALK− and DUSP22 rearrangement may be present.

PCALCL_staging.system: TNM classification for cutaneous lymphoma other than MF/SS (CUTB-A) [5]
PCALCL_staging.m_stages: As per PCFCL staging (M0, M1, MX)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Excellent prognosis; treat with local RT or excision.
Good prognosis; may need systemic therapy.
Guarded prognosis; treat with systemic therapy ± RT.

staging_pearls: Spontaneous regression is common.
Distinguish from systemic ALCL based on ALK expression.

LyP_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Lymphomatoid papulosis (LyP) presents with chronic, recurrent, spontaneously regressing papulonodular skin lesions. Lesions are often grouped or generalized and may occur at any age. Histologically, it shows large atypical cells in an inflammatory background. LyP is considered a lymphoproliferative disorder with excellent prognosis but associated with increased risk of other lymphomas (e.g., MF, PC-ALCL) [1,13].
atypical_or_missed: May be confused with insect bites, folliculitis, or other dermatoses.
Overlap with PC-ALCL or MF.

LyP_red_flags: Development of persistent or large lesions

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: Polymorphous infiltrate with large CD30+ cells; may show different histologic subtypes [1].

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm CD30 expression and rule out other lymphomas.
diagnostic.key_findings: CD3, CD4, CD8, CD20, CD30, CD56, ALK [1].

diagnostic.rationale: May be present in LyP.

staging.rationale: LyP is a cutaneous disorder without systemic involvement.

biomarkers.rationale: May be clonal but not diagnostic alone.

LyP_staging.system: Not typically staged as it is a cutaneous disorder.
LyP_staging.stage_groups: None; prognosis is excellent with 5-year OS >95%.
staging_pearls: Life-long follow-up due to risk of second lymphoid malignancies.
Histologic subtypes (A, B, C, D, E) may have different associations.

SPTCL_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma (SPTCL) presents with deep subcutaneous nodules or plaques, typically on the legs, but can involve other areas. Lesions are non-ulcerated and may resolve with lipodystrophy and hyperpigmentation. Systemic symptoms (fever, malaise) are common, and hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is a serious complication. SPTCL occurs in younger patients and is associated with autoimmune disorders. It is indolent in most cases but HLH worsens prognosis [1,14].

symptoms.name: Fever, malaise, weight loss
symptoms.detail: B symptoms, more common with HLH.

atypical_or_missed: May be mistaken for lupus panniculitis or other panniculitis.
HLH may dominate the clinical picture.

diagnostic.test: Deep subcutaneous skin biopsy (wedge or deep punch)
diagnostic.rationale: Essential; must include adipose tissue.
diagnostic.key_findings: Lobular panniculitis-like infiltrate of atypical CD8+ T cells rimming adipocytes; immunophenotype: CD3+, CD8+, βF1+, CD2+, CD5+, CD7+ [1,14].

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm diagnosis and rule out gamma/delta lymphoma.
diagnostic.key_findings: TCR beta, TCR delta, CD2, CD3, CD20, CD4, CD5, CD7, CD8, CD30, CD56 [1].

diagnostic.rationale: To rule out EBV-associated lymphomas.
diagnostic.key_findings: Negative in SPTCL [1].

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm clonality.
diagnostic.key_findings: Clonal TRB/TRG rearrangements [1].

staging.rationale: To assess extent of subcutaneous involvement and exclude extracutaneous disease.
To evaluate for HLH and baseline.

biomarkers.rationale: Associated with HLH and may guide therapy.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis or if HLH present

do_not_miss: EBER to rule out EBV+ lymphomas

SPTCL_staging.system: TNM classification for cutaneous lymphoma other than MF/SS (CUTB-A) [5]
SPTCL_staging.t_stages: Not directly applicable; extent of subcutaneous involvement is described.
SPTCL_staging.stage_groups: Not formally staged; prognosis based on presence of HLH and tumor burden.
staging_pearls: HLH is a major adverse prognostic factor.
Limited subcutaneous disease without HLH has excellent prognosis.

PCSM_TLPD_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Primary cutaneous CD4+ small/medium T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder (PCSM-TLPD) presents as a solitary, asymptomatic nodule or plaque, commonly on the head and neck, trunk, or upper extremities. It follows an indolent course with frequent spontaneous regression and excellent prognosis (5-year OS 100%). Extracutaneous progression is absent [1,15].
atypical_or_missed: May be misdiagnosed as reactive lymphoid hyperplasia or other cutaneous lymphomas.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: Band-like or nodular infiltrate of small/medium CD4+ T cells with follicular helper phenotype: CD3+, CD4+, CD8−, PD-1+, ICOS+, BCL6−/+ [1,15].

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm phenotype.
diagnostic.key_findings: CD3, CD4, CD8, PD-1, ICOS, BCL6, CXCL13 [1].

diagnostic.rationale: Clonal in most cases.
diagnostic.key_findings: Clonal TRB/TRG rearrangements [1].

staging.rationale: Usually sufficient; imaging not routinely needed.

PCSM_TLPD_staging.system: No formal staging system; clinical assessment.
staging_pearls: Spontaneous regression is common.
No extracutaneous progression.

PCA_TLPD_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Primary cutaneous CD8+ acral T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder (PCA-TLPD) presents as a solitary asymptomatic papule, nodule, or plaque, typically on acral sites (ear, nose). It follows an indolent course with excellent prognosis (5-year OS 100%). Spontaneous regression can occur but is less common than in PCSM-TLPD [1,16].
atypical_or_missed: May be mistaken for benign acral lesions.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: Dermal infiltrate of atypical medium-sized CD8+ T cells; immunophenotype: CD3+, CD4−, CD8+, TIA1+, granzyme B+/−, EBV− [1,16].

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm cytotoxic phenotype.
diagnostic.key_findings: CD3, CD4, CD8, TIA1, granzyme B [1].

diagnostic.rationale: Clonal in most cases.
diagnostic.key_findings: Clonal TRB/TRG rearrangements [1].

PCA_TLPD_staging.system: No formal staging system; clinical assessment.
staging_pearls: Acral location is characteristic.
No extracutaneous progression.

management_principles.overview: The management of cutaneous lymphomas requires a multidisciplinary approach involving dermatology, pathology, hematology/oncology, and radiation oncology. Treatment goals are individualized and often focus on controlling symptoms, reducing disease burden, and minimizing progression risk, as most therapies (except allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant [HCT]) are not curative. Therapeutic decisions are guided by disease subtype, stage, clinical presentation, and prognostic factors. For indolent entities like mycosis fungoides (MF) and primary cutaneous B-cell lymphomas (PCBCL), skin-directed therapies are preferred for limited disease, while systemic therapy is reserved for advanced or refractory cases. Aggressive subtypes such as primary cutaneous diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (PCDLBCL), leg type, or transformed MF require prompt systemic chemoimmunotherapy. Supportive care for symptom management, particularly pruritus and infection prevention, is integral throughout the disease course.

by_intent.approach: Prioritize therapies with favorable toxicity profiles (e.g., skin-directed therapies, topical agents) to improve quality of life without significant cumulative toxicity. Maintenance or tapering of regimens may be considered for patients with clinical benefit to optimize response duration.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with extensive or high-risk disease
by_intent.approach: Use systemic agents (e.g., mogamulizumab, brentuximab vedotin) or combinations (e.g., chemoimmunotherapy for PCDLBCL, leg type) to reduce disease burden and delay progression. Allogeneic HCT may be considered for eligible patients with refractory advanced-stage MF/SS.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: A multidisciplinary team (MDT) with expertise in cutaneous lymphomas is optimal, especially for advanced or complex cases. This includes specialists in dermatology, pathology (with expertise in cutaneous lymphoma), hematology/oncology, and radiation oncology. Referral to specialized centers is preferred for rare subtypes or when treatment considerations are complex.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) influences treatment tolerance and modality selection. Patients with good PS (ECOG 0-1) may be candidates for more intensive therapies (e.g., combination chemotherapy, HCT). Those with poor PS (ECOG ≥2) may benefit from less aggressive approaches (e.g., single-agent systemic therapy, supportive care).

management_pathways.title: Primary Cutaneous Indolent T-Cell Lymphoproliferative Disorders (PCSM-TLPD and PCA-TLPD)

options.label: Clinical observation, topical/intralesional steroids, low-dose RT, or surgical excision

items.text: Biopsy of suspicious skin lesions (punch, incisional, or excisional preferred over shave biopsy) with review by a pathologist with expertise
Adequate immunophenotyping to establish diagnosis (IHC panel: CD20, CD3, CD10, BCL2, BCL6, IRF4/MUM1 for B-cell; CD2, CD3, CD4, CD5, CD7, CD8, CD20, CD30 for T-cell)
Molecular analysis to detect clonal TRB and TRG gene rearrangements (for T-cell lymphomas)
Complete history and physical examination including complete skin assessment (% BSA, lesion type), palpation of peripheral lymph nodes, and assessment for organomegaly
Complete blood count (CBC) with differential
Comprehensive metabolic panel
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)
Assessment for distress

Integrated whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or C/A/P CT with contrast for ≥T2b or LCT or FMF, or with palpable adenopathy or abnormal laboratory studies; consider for T2a or otherwise clinically indicated
For PCBCL: C/A/P CT with contrast and/or FDG-PET/CT (may be omitted if clinically indicated for indolent subtypes)
For PCTLD (PC-ALCL): C/A/P CT with contrast or integrated whole-body FDG-PET/CT
Flow cytometry to assess and quantitate expanded T-cell population with aberrant phenotype (optional for T1 MF)
Clonal TRB and TRG gene rearrangement in peripheral blood lymphocytes if blood involvement suspected
HTLV-1/2 serology (if clinically indicated)
For PCMZL: SPEP/quantitative immunoglobulins
For PCDLBCL, leg type: hepatitis B and C testing
Biopsy of enlarged lymph nodes or suspected extracutaneous sites (excisional or adequate core needle preferred; FNA alone insufficient)
Bone marrow biopsy (optional for solitary cutaneous ALCL or without extracutaneous involvement; considered for unexplained cytopenias or clinical suspicion of aggressive subtypes)
Assessment of HTLV-1/2 serology
Pregnancy testing in patients of childbearing potential if contemplating treatments contraindicated in pregnancy
Discuss fertility preservation options (sperm banking, IVF, ovarian tissue or oocyte cryopreservation)
For SPTCL: HLH workup if B symptoms or HLH suspected (including ferritin)
For SPTCL: germline testing for HAVCR2 mutation
For indolent LPD (PCSM-TLPD, PCA-TLPD): Imaging can be considered to exclude systemic lymphoma if clinically indicated

surgery.role: Limited role in cutaneous lymphomas; primarily for diagnosis (biopsy) and for localized, small lesions where excision is feasible and non-disfiguring.
principles: Surgical excision may be considered for solitary, small lesions of indolent PCBCL (PCMZL, PCFCL) or for localized PC-ALCL when RT is not desired or feasible.
Excision is generally not curative and is associated with high recurrence rates, especially for indolent subtypes.
Surgical margin should be minimal to avoid disfigurement, especially in cosmetically sensitive areas.

procedures.name: Surgical excision
procedures.approach: Simple excision with minimal margins (1-2 cm) for small, solitary lesions.
procedures.indications: Small, solitary lesions of PCMZL, PCFCL, or PC-ALCL where RT is not preferred.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Lesions must be solitary, small, and located in areas where excision is feasible without significant morbidity or disfigurement. Not recommended for multifocal, large, or anatomically challenging lesions.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Standard pre-operative evaluation; ensure adequate biopsy has been performed for diagnosis.

radiation_therapy.role: Highly effective for local control of cutaneous lymphomas; used with curative intent for limited disease and palliatively for symptomatic lesions.
principles: Radiation therapy (RT) is extremely radiosensitive in cutaneous lymphomas.
Involved-site radiation therapy (ISRT) is the standard approach, targeting clinically evident disease with adequate margins (1-2 cm circumferentially and in depth).
Dose and fractionation depend on disease subtype, extent, and intent (curative vs. palliative).
RT can be used alone for limited disease or combined with skin-directed or systemic therapies for more extensive disease.

dose_frameworks.name: Curative RT for indolent PCBCL (PCMZL, PCFCL)
Palliative RT for MF/SS
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: Up to 8 Gy in a single fraction; lower doses per fraction (3-5 Gy) may be preferred
dose_frameworks.indication: Individual patches, plaques, or tumors for symptom control

dose_frameworks.name: RT for PC-ALCL
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 24 Gy (curative); doses as low as 6 Gy used

dose_frameworks.name: Total skin electron beam therapy (TSEBT)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 12-36 Gy (conventional dose ≥30 Gy; low dose <30 Gy)

approaches.name: ISRT for cutaneous lesions
approaches.dose_fractionation: Conventional fractionation (2 Gy per fraction) or hypofractionated regimens
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not typically used concurrently for cutaneous lymphomas
approaches.indication: Localized patches, plaques, tumors of MF/SS; PCBCL; PC-ALCL
approaches.toxicities: Skin erythema, desquamation, fatigue; long-term risks include skin atrophy, telangiectasia, and secondary malignancies (low risk with modern techniques)

approaches.name: TSEBT
approaches.dose_fractionation: 12 Gy over 2-3 weeks (low-dose) or 30-36 Gy over 6-9 weeks (conventional)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Limited safety data for concurrent systemic retinoids, HDAC inhibitors, or mogamulizumab
approaches.toxicities: Skin erythema, desquamation, dryness, edema; eye, hand, foot shielding required; temporary alopecia; nail changes; fatigue

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used for advanced-stage disease, inadequate response to skin-directed therapy, or aggressive subtypes. Treatment is often continuous or maintenance to control disease, as durable remissions off treatment are uncommon. Selection depends on disease subtype, stage, prior therapies, comorbidities, and patient preferences. Single agents are preferred over combination chemotherapy due to lower cumulative toxicity.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: MF/SS - Early stage (IB-IIA) with inadequate response to skin-directed therapy
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Bexarotene, brentuximab vedotin, interferon alfa, methotrexate, mogamulizumab, romidepsin, vorinostat
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Lower toxicity profiles; can be combined with skin-directed therapy
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Acitretin, denileukin diftitox-cxdl, ECP, interferon gamma-1b, isotretinoin, alemtuzumab, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, pembrolizumab, pralatrexate
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: CD30 expression for brentuximab vedotin; CCR4 expression not required for mogamulizumab

by_stage_and_setting.setting: MF/SS - Advanced stage (≥IIB) or refractory to skin-directed therapy
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Brentuximab vedotin, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, pralatrexate, romidepsin, or combination with skin-directed therapy
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Higher efficacy for advanced disease; can be combined with skin-directed therapy to maximize response
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Bexarotene, brentuximab vedotin, denileukin diftitox-cxdl, interferon alfa, methotrexate, mogamulizumab, pembrolizumab, multiagent chemotherapy regimens (for refractory disease)
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: CD30 for brentuximab vedotin; HTLV-1 testing (rapid progression reported with pembrolizumab in HTLV+ patients)

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Systemic therapy (brentuximab vedotin, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, pralatrexate, romidepsin) ± RT for local control
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: LCT often indicates more aggressive disease requiring systemic therapy
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Pembrolizumab, multiagent chemotherapy regimens (for refractory disease)
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: CD30 expression may guide use of brentuximab vedotin

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Mogamulizumab (high burden), romidepsin (high burden), bexarotene, ECP, interferon alfa, methotrexate, vorinostat
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Higher efficacy in blood compartment with mogamulizumab; ECP appropriate for blood involvement
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Alemtuzumab, brentuximab vedotin, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, pembrolizumab, pralatrexate
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: CCR4 expression not required for mogamulizumab
MYD88 and CD79B mutations (associated with inferior prognosis)

by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Methotrexate (≤50 mg weekly), systemic retinoids (bexarotene), pralatrexate, interferon
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: CD30 expression (though responses seen across all levels)

by_stage_and_setting.setting: SPTCL - With HLH or high tumor burden
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Etoposide-based combination chemotherapy (to control HLH) followed by disease-specific therapy (cyclosporine, pralatrexate, romidepsin)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Control HLH first, then target lymphoma
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: HAVCR2 germline mutations (may confer resistance to CHOP)

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment for Cutaneous Lymphomas
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response should be assessed after completion of induction therapy or at regular intervals during maintenance therapy. For MF/SS, assessment includes clinical evaluation of skin, blood, lymph nodes, and viscera. Imaging is indicated when there is suspicion of extracutaneous disease or progression.
response_logic: Patients achieving a clinical benefit or responding to primary treatment should be considered for maintenance or tapering of regimens to optimize response duration.
Disease relapse (with the same stage) after discontinuation of therapy often responds well to retreatment with previous therapy.
Patients with persistent disease should be treated with other primary treatment options not received before to improve response before moving to treatment for refractory disease.
Response criteria for MF/SS are not standardized and decisions to continue or switch therapy are often based on clinical assessment of the dominant disease compartment and symptom burden.
For PCBCL, additional imaging during treatment is not needed after negative initial staging; FDG-PET/CT or CT at end of treatment may assess response.
For PCTLD, response is assessed clinically; relapsed disease often responds well to the same treatment.
For SPTCL, response is assessed clinically and with imaging (PET/CT); maintenance therapy may be considered for patients with clinical benefit.
imaging_recommendations: For MF/SS: Imaging (FDG-PET/CT or C/A/P CT) is indicated when there is suspicion of extracutaneous disease or clinical progression.
For PCBCL: FDG-PET/CT or C/A/P CT at end of treatment may be needed to assess response or if clinical suspicion of progressive disease.
For PCTLD (PC-ALCL): Repeat imaging as clinically indicated based on distribution of disease.
For SPTCL: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT to assess extent of extracutaneous involvement before and after treatment.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Rebiopsy skin if suspicious of LCT or FMF (not previously confirmed pathologically) or aggressive clinical behavior.
Biopsy of enlarged lymph nodes or extracutaneous sites if clinical suspicion of disease progression.
For refractory disease, consider clinical trials or allogeneic HCT in eligible patients.
Mogamulizumab-associated skin rash should be biopsied to rule out disease progression.

tier_2_handbook.prognosis: The prognosis for cutaneous lymphomas varies significantly by disease subtype, stage, and histologic features. For primary cutaneous B-cell lymphomas (PC-BCL), primary cutaneous follicle center lymphoma (PCFCL) and primary cutaneous marginal zone lymphoma (PCMZL) are indolent, with 5-year overall survival (OS) rates exceeding 95% and 99%, respectively [1,2]. In contrast, primary cutaneous diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, leg type (PC-DLBCL, leg type) is aggressive, with a 5-year OS rate of approximately 50% [2,3]. For mycosis fungoides (MF) and Sézary syndrome (SS), prognosis is highly dependent on clinical stage: patients with early-stage disease (IA-IB) have an excellent prognosis with 5-year OS rates >90%, while advanced-stage disease (IIB-IV) carries a poorer prognosis, with 5-year OS rates ranging from 28% to 68% based on prognostic models [4]. Prognostic factors include age, T classification, presence of extracutaneous disease, extent of blood involvement, elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and large cell transformation (LCT) [4,5]. LCT is an independent adverse prognostic factor; in one analysis of 117 patients with SS, median OS was 35 months for those with LCT versus 80 months without LCT [6]. For primary cutaneous CD30+ T-cell lymphoproliferative disorders, primary cutaneous anaplastic large cell lymphoma (PC-ALCL) has a favorable prognosis with 5-year OS rates of 81%–91%, while lymphomatoid papulosis (LyP) is benign with a 5-year OS rate of 92% [7,8]. Subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma (SPTCL) has a 5-year OS rate of 80%, but this drops to approximately 46% when complicated by hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) [9]. Primary cutaneous indolent T-cell lymphoproliferative disorders (PCSM-TLPD and PCA-TLPD) have an excellent prognosis with 5-year OS rates of 100% [10].
tier_2_handbook.complications: Disease-related complications in cutaneous lymphomas include pruritus, which affects nearly 90% of patients with MF/SS and significantly impairs quality of life [11]. Infections are frequent, particularly cutaneous bacterial infections (e.g., Staphylococcus aureus) and viral infections (e.g., herpes simplex virus), which can lead to bacteremia or sepsis, especially in erythrodermic patients [12]. In SPTCL, HLH is a serious complication occurring in 15%–25% of cases and is associated with high mortality [9]. LyP is associated with an increased risk of secondary lymphomas, including MF and PC-ALCL [13]. Treatment-related complications vary by therapy. Chemotherapy can cause myelosuppression, mucositis, and increased infection risk. Immunotherapy with mogamulizumab is associated with infusion-related reactions (37%), skin eruptions (25%), and diarrhea (14%), with grade 3 pyrexia (4%) and cellulitis (3%) [14]. Brentuximab vedotin, an anti-CD30 antibody-drug conjugate, commonly causes peripheral neuropathy (69% of patients), which usually resolves or improves over time [15]. Bexarotene frequently causes hypertriglyceridemia (79%), hypercholesterolemia (71%), and hypothyroidism (52%), requiring laboratory monitoring [16]. Alemtuzumab, an anti-CD52 antibody, carries a high risk of cytomegalovirus (CMV) reactivation and opportunistic infections, necessitating prophylaxis [17]. Radiation therapy (RT) can cause skin irritation, erythema, and long-term risks of secondary skin malignancies, especially with cumulative ultraviolet exposure from phototherapy [18]. Surgery is rarely discussed but may lead to wound healing complications.
tier_2_handbook.supportive_care: Supportive care for cutaneous lymphomas focuses on symptom management and infection prevention. Pruritus, a common and distressing symptom in MF/SS, is managed with topical corticosteroids, moisturizers, and systemic agents. First-line options include H1 antihistamines (single-agent or combination) and gabapentin. Second-line treatments include aprepitant, mirtazapine, or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). Third-line options include naltrexone or systemic steroids [11]. Infection prevention is critical, especially in erythrodermic patients. Measures include maintaining skin barrier with moisturizers, diluted bleach baths (1/2 cup bleach in a full tub, 5–10 minutes, 2–3 times weekly), and prophylactic intranasal mupirocin for Staphylococcus aureus carriers. For patients with frequent herpes simplex virus (HSV) recurrence, acyclovir prophylaxis is recommended. Patients receiving alemtuzumab require CMV monitoring (quantitative PCR every 2–3 weeks) and pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) prophylaxis with sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim [17]. Brentuximab vedotin carries a risk of John Cunningham (JC) virus reactivation and progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), which is usually fatal; patients should be educated on symptoms such as confusion, dizziness, and vision problems [19]. Nutritional support, anti-emetic protocols, GCSF guidance, VTE prophylaxis, pain management, psychosocial support, and dental care are not explicitly detailed in the guidelines but are integral to comprehensive care for lymphoma patients. Pain management may include topical or systemic analgesics, and psychosocial support should address the chronic nature of the disease and treatment burden.
tier_2_handbook.follow_up: Follow-up for cutaneous lymphomas emphasizes regular clinical assessment and monitoring for disease progression or relapse. For indolent PC-BCL (PCFCL and PCMZL), after initial treatment, observation is recommended with no routine imaging unless clinically indicated. Imaging (FDG-PET/CT or C/A/P CT) may be repeated at the end of treatment to assess response or if there is clinical suspicion of progression [20]. For MF/SS, follow-up includes periodic skin examinations, assessment of body surface area (BSA) involvement, and monitoring for extracutaneous disease. Patients with advanced-stage disease or those on systemic therapy require more frequent follow-up. The guidelines do not specify a fixed follow-up schedule, but it is generally recommended to assess response and manage symptoms at each visit. For LyP, lifelong follow-up with thorough skin examination is warranted due to the high risk of second lymphoid malignancies (e.g., MF, PC-ALCL) [21]. Late effects screening should include monitoring for treatment-related toxicities (e.g., neuropathy from brentuximab vedotin, metabolic effects from bexarotene) and secondary malignancies, particularly skin cancers in patients who have received phototherapy or radiation. Recurrence patterns vary by disease: PC-BCL relapses are common in the skin (50% for PCMZL, 30% for PCFCL) but rarely extracutaneous [2]. MF/SS relapses are frequent after therapy discontinuation, and decisions to continue or switch therapy are guided by clinical assessment of the dominant disease compartment [22].
tier_2_handbook.surveillance: Surveillance for cutaneous lymphomas involves clinical follow-up, imaging, laboratory monitoring, and supportive care. Clinical follow-up schedules are not standardized but are tailored to disease subtype and stage. For indolent PC-BCL, regular skin exams and palpation of lymph nodes are sufficient, with imaging only if clinically indicated. For MF/SS, follow-up includes complete skin examination, palpation of peripheral lymph nodes, and assessment of organomegaly at each visit. Laboratory monitoring includes complete blood count (CBC) with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). For patients with blood involvement (B1 or B2), flow cytometry to quantitate abnormal T-cells is recommended, along with clonal T-cell receptor (TCR) gene rearrangement studies if blood involvement is suspected [22]. Imaging strategy: integrated whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or C/A/P CT with contrast is recommended for staging in MF/SS for patients with T3/T4 disease, LCT, folliculotropic MF, palpable adenopathy, or abnormal laboratory studies. For PC-ALCL, C/A/P CT or FDG-PET/CT is recommended at diagnosis. In LyP, imaging is only done if there is suspicion of systemic involvement by an associated lymphoma [21]. Supportive follow-up includes assessment of pruritus, infection risk, and quality of life. Patients on phototherapy should be monitored for actinic keratoses, non-melanoma skin cancers, and melanoma. For patients on alemtuzumab, CMV PCR monitoring every 2–3 weeks is essential [17].

special_situations.key_message: Many skin-directed and systemic therapies are contraindicated or of unknown safety in pregnancy.
special_situations.management: Pregnancy testing is recommended for patients of childbearing potential before initiating therapy. Fertility preservation options (sperm banking, in vitro fertilization, oocyte or ovarian tissue cryopreservation) should be discussed. Treatment decisions should be made with a multidisciplinary team, prioritizing safe therapies for mother and fetus.
special_situations.evidence: Based on NCCN guidelines and general oncology principles.

special_situations.key_message: Cutaneous lymphomas in children are rare, and treatment data are limited.
special_situations.management: Therapy should be individualized, often mirroring adult approaches but with consideration of growth and development. Topical therapies, low-dose RT, and carefully selected systemic agents may be used. Bexarotene gel is noted as useful in pediatric patients in some case reports.
special_situations.evidence: Case reports and small series; no prospective trials specifically for pediatric cutaneous lymphoma.

special_situations.key_message: HIV infection may be associated with certain cutaneous lymphomas, and antiretroviral therapy should be coordinated with lymphoma treatment.
special_situations.management: Consultation with infectious disease and oncology is recommended. Some lymphomas in HIV are aggressive and may require modified regimens.
special_situations.evidence: Guidelines for HIV-associated lymphomas and expert opinion.

special_situations.key_message: HTLV-1 infection can cause adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL), which may present with cutaneous lesions.
special_situations.management: HTLV-1 serology should be assessed, especially in endemic areas. ATLL requires specific treatment protocols different from primary cutaneous lymphomas.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN guidelines for T-cell lymphomas and HTLV-1 prevalence data.

special_situations.key_message: LCT often signals a more aggressive disease course and may require a shift to systemic therapies.
special_situations.management: Rebiopsy if LCT is suspected. For localized LCT, RT may be considered. For generalized LCT, systemic therapy (e.g., brentuximab vedotin, romidepsin, or multiagent chemotherapy) is recommended, often followed by allogeneic HCT if eligible.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN guidelines for MF/SS and expert consensus.

special_situations.key_message: Refractory disease to multiple prior therapies warrants consideration of clinical trials or allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT).
special_situations.management: Participation in clinical trials is encouraged. Allogeneic HCT may be curative for eligible patients with advanced-stage MF/SS, but the risks must be carefully weighed.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN guidelines and retrospective studies showing survival benefit with allogeneic HCT in selected patients.

clinical_pearls: PCFCL and PCMZL are indolent lymphomas with excellent prognosis; treatment is often conservative with skin-directed therapies like low-dose involved-site RT (ISRT).
PC-DLBCL, leg type is aggressive and requires systemic chemoimmunotherapy (e.g., R-CHOP) due to high risk of extracutaneous relapse.
MF/SS is not curable with conventional therapy; treatment goals are palliative, focusing on symptom control and quality of life.
Brentuximab vedotin is effective for CD30-positive MF/SS and PC-ALCL, but watch for peripheral neuropathy.
Mogamulizumab is particularly effective in the blood compartment and is preferred for Sézary syndrome with high tumor burden.
Allogeneic HCT offers potential long-term survival for select patients with advanced MF/SS, especially those with erythrodermic disease or SS.
In SPTCL, immunomodulatory therapies (e.g., cyclosporine) are preferred over chemotherapy for patients without HLH.
LyP is a chronic, self-regressing condition; treatment is reserved for symptomatic patients to minimize morbidity.

key_trials.full_name: Brentuximab vedotin or physician's choice in CD30-positive cutaneous T-cell lymphoma
key_trials.population: Patients with previously treated CD30-positive MF (≥ stage IB) or PC-ALCL.
key_trials.intervention: Brentuximab vedotin 1.8 mg/kg every 3 weeks for up to 16 cycles.
key_trials.comparator: Methotrexate (5–50 mg weekly) or bexarotene (300 mg/m2 daily) for 48 weeks.
key_trials.key_result: ORR4 was 55% for brentuximab vedotin vs. 13% for physician's choice (p<0.0001). Median PFS was 17 months vs. 4 months (HR 0.270).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Complete response rate, duration of response, patient-reported symptom burden.
key_trials.practice_change: Established brentuximab vedotin as preferred therapy for CD30-positive MF/SS and PC-ALCL.

key_trials.full_name: Mogamulizumab versus vorinostat in previously treated cutaneous T-cell lymphoma
key_trials.population: Patients with relapsed or refractory MF (≥ stage IB) and SS.
key_trials.intervention: Mogamulizumab 1 mg/kg IV weekly for 4 weeks, then days 1 and 15 of subsequent cycles.
key_trials.primary_endpoint: ORR as assessed by independent review committee (IRC).
key_trials.key_result: ORR was 28% for mogamulizumab vs. 5% for vorinostat (p<0.0001). Median PFS was 8 months vs. 3 months (HR 0.53).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Response rates by disease compartment (skin, blood, lymph nodes), duration of response.
key_trials.practice_change: Established mogamulizumab as a preferred therapy for relapsed/refractory MF/SS, particularly with blood involvement.

key_trials.full_name: Efficacy and safety of denileukin diftitox-cxdl in relapsed or refractory cutaneous T-cell lymphoma
key_trials.population: Patients with relapsed or refractory MF and SS (≥1 prior systemic therapy; CD25-positive).
key_trials.intervention: Denileukin diftitox (reformulated) 9 mcg/kg/day IV for 5 days every 21 days for up to 8 cycles.
key_trials.key_result: ORR was 36% (95% CI, 25–48). Median duration of response was 9 months.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Reduction in skin disease burden, safety profile.
key_trials.practice_change: Supported FDA approval of denileukin diftitox for relapsed/refractory MF/SS.
